result:
:::writing
The town had a clock that no one trusted.

It stood at the far end of Market Street, mounted high in a brick tower that used to belong to a textile mill before the machines went quiet and the pigeons moved in. The clock had four faces, one for each direction, as if it wanted to reassure everyone at once. But the hands never agreed. The north face ran slow, the south face ran fast, and the east and west seemed to take turns drifting in and out of alignment like distracted dancers.

Visitors noticed it immediately. “Which one is right?” they’d ask, shading their eyes, turning in a slow circle. The locals would shrug.

“Depends what you’re late for,” someone would say.

There were theories, of course. Old Mr. Delaney claimed the clock had been struck by lightning in 1972 and never recovered its senses. The librarian insisted that the gears were warped during a heatwave that softened the metal just enough to make it stubborn. A group of teenagers once spread a rumor that each face was connected to a different year, and if you stared long enough, you could slip into whichever time you chose. They dared each other to try, then ran off laughing when the bells tolled unevenly, as if the tower itself couldn’t decide how many times to speak.

But the truth—if there was one—was less dramatic and more peculiar.

Elias Rook had been the clockkeeper for nearly forty years. No one had hired him. No one remembered when he started. He simply appeared one spring with a ring of keys and a habit of climbing the narrow staircase inside the tower every morning at dawn. He wore the same gray coat regardless of season, and he moved with the careful precision of someone who believed the world might shatter if he made a sudden gesture.

Children were told not to bother him, which of course ensured that they did.

“Why don’t you fix it?” they would ask, peering up at him as he emerged from the tower, hands blackened with oil.

Elias would pause, considering the question as though it were new each time.

“It works,” he would say.

“But it’s wrong.”

He would tilt his head, glancing up at the mismatched faces.

“Is it?” he’d reply, and then he’d walk away.

The town adjusted, as towns do. Bakers opened when the east face suggested it was morning enough. Shopkeepers closed when the west face grew dim and the shadows stretched long. Schoolchildren learned to measure their days not by the clock but by the rhythm of bells, which rang in uneven intervals that somehow felt more honest than a steady chime.

Life went on, slightly out of sync, like a song played on an instrument that refused to stay perfectly tuned.

Then Mara came back.

She arrived on a bus that hissed like it was relieved to be done with the journey. The driver didn’t bother announcing the stop; he just jerked his thumb toward the window, where the crooked clock tower leaned into the sky like an old man trying to remember something important.

Mara stepped off with a single suitcase and a look that suggested she wasn’t sure if she’d made a mistake.

She hadn’t been home in twelve years. Not since the summer her father died and the house became too quiet to bear. She’d left for the city with a scholarship, a plan, and a determination to build a life that didn’t depend on the unpredictable heartbeat of a small town.

She had succeeded, in the way people do when they are very good at pretending they aren’t lonely.

But success, she’d learned, had a way of thinning out over time. The edges frayed. The colors faded. One day you woke up and realized that everything you had built felt oddly weightless, like a set of painted walls that might topple if you leaned on them too hard.

So she came back.

The house was still there, of course. It had been rented out, neglected, reclaimed, and finally left to settle into a kind of weary patience. The porch sagged. The paint peeled. The garden had surrendered to a tangle of weeds that seemed to have their own ambitions.

Inside, the air smelled like dust and old wood and something faintly sweet, like a memory that hadn’t quite evaporated.

Mara set her suitcase down and stood in the doorway, listening.

There it was.

The bells.

They rang from the tower in a pattern she didn’t recognize—three quick notes, a pause, then two more, slightly off. It wasn’t the clean, predictable chime she remembered from childhood. It sounded… hesitant. As if the clock were asking a question instead of making a statement.

She found herself walking toward it without quite deciding to go.

Market Street hadn’t changed much. The same bakery, the same bookstore, the same narrow café with its perpetually fogged windows. People glanced at her, some with recognition flickering across their faces, others with polite curiosity.

And at the end of it all, the tower.

Up close, it looked older than she remembered. The bricks were darker, the mortar more fragile. The clock faces were smudged with grime, but the hands were still visible, pointing in their stubborn, contradictory directions.

“It’s worse than it used to be,” she said aloud, not realizing she’d spoken until someone answered.

“Or better.”

She turned.

Elias Rook stood a few steps away, his gray coat catching the light in a way that made it seem almost silver. He looked exactly as she remembered, which was unsettling. People weren’t supposed to remain unchanged while the rest of the world shifted around them.

“You’re still here,” she said.

“So are you,” he replied.

She glanced back at the clock.

“It’s wrong,” she said, echoing the certainty of her younger self.

Elias studied her for a moment.

“Come see,” he said, gesturing toward the tower door.

She hesitated, then followed.

The staircase was narrow and spiraled tightly, the air growing cooler as they climbed. The sounds of the town faded, replaced by the steady, uneven ticking of the clock’s inner workings.

At the top, the mechanism revealed itself in a tangle of gears and pendulums, each moving at its own pace. Some spun quickly, others crept along with deliberate slowness. A few seemed to pause entirely before lurching forward again.

“It’s not broken,” Elias said, watching her take it in. “It’s… listening.”

“To what?” she asked.

“To everything,” he said simply.

He stepped closer to the machinery, placing a hand on one of the larger gears. It shifted slightly under his touch, as if acknowledging him.

“People think time is one thing,” he continued. “A straight line. A steady march. But that’s just a convenience. A story we tell so we don’t get lost.”

Mara frowned.

“And this?” she gestured at the chaotic motion.

“This is closer to the truth,” he said. “Time stretches. Contracts. It stumbles. It races. It lingers where it’s needed and slips away where it’s not.”

“That doesn’t make any sense.”

“It doesn’t have to,” he said gently. “It just has to work.”

She looked at the gears again, then back at him.

“And it does?”

He smiled, just barely.

“Tell me,” he said, “when you were away… did time feel the same as it did here?”

She opened her mouth, then paused.

In the city, days had blurred together, each one slipping into the next with a kind of relentless efficiency. Weeks vanished without leaving much behind. But there had also been moments—small, unexpected ones—that seemed to expand, filling entire afternoons with a single conversation, a single glance, a single breath.

“No,” she admitted.

Elias nodded.

“The clock just makes that visible,” he said.

They stood in silence for a while, the uneven ticking wrapping around them like a conversation they didn’t need to join.

Finally, Mara exhaled.

“So you’re not going to fix it,” she said.

Elias shook his head.

“It isn’t asking to be fixed.”

She considered that.

Outside, the bells began to ring again—this time in a pattern that felt almost deliberate, like a rhythm she might learn if she listened long enough.

Mara smiled, just a little.

“Okay,” she said.

And for the first time in years, she didn’t check the time.
:::writing

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
